title: Prompt Injection Attacks on Large Language Models
summary: First systematic study of prompt injection. Categorizes attacks into direct (instruction override) and indirect (data channel injection). Demonstrates that all tested LLMs are vulnerable to simple injection attacks. Proposes instruction hierarchy as a defense.
key_findings: Direct prompt injection succeeds against all tested models with trivial prompts
Indirect injection via web pages and emails is highly effective
Instruction hierarchy (system > user > data) is the most promising defense

title: Not What You've Signed Up For: Compromising Real-World LLM-Integrated Applications with Indirect Prompt Injection
summary: Demonstrates indirect prompt injection attacks on LLM-integrated applications (Bing Chat, code assistants). Shows that hidden instructions in web pages, PDFs, and videos can hijack LLM behavior.
key_findings: LLM-integrated apps that read external content are vulnerable to injection via that content
Attackers can exfiltrate conversation data via markdown image injection
Multi-stage attacks can persist across sessions

title: Universal and Transferable Adversarial Attacks on Aligned Language Models
summary: Develops an automated method (GCG) to generate adversarial suffixes that jailbreak aligned LLMs. Demonstrates transferability across models including Vicuna, LLaMA, and GPT-3.5/4.
key_findings: Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) can automatically discover jailbreak suffixes
Adversarial suffixes transfer across different model architectures
Adding adversarial suffixes to otherwise refused prompts achieves high success rates

title: Jailbroken: How Does LLM Safety Training Fail?
summary: Systematic analysis of jailbreak failure modes. Identifies two main categories: competing objectives (where the model's helpfulness conflicts with safety) and mismatched generalization (where safety training doesn't generalize to new input formats).
key_findings: Jailbreaks exploit competition between helpfulness and safety objectives
Safety training fails to generalize to encoded inputs (Base64, leetspeak)
Multi-shot prompting can override safety training

title: Ignore Previous Prompt: Attack Techniques for Language Models
summary: Comprehensive taxonomy of prompt injection attacks. Covers 29 distinct attack techniques across 8 categories. Includes the Gandalf challenge as an evaluation benchmark.
key_findings: Taxonomy of 29 prompt injection techniques across 8 categories
Gandalf serves as a practical benchmark for evaluating injection defenses
Most production systems remain vulnerable to basic injection techniques

title: Red-Teaming Language Models and Language Model-Integrated Systems
summary: Large-scale red teaming methodology for LLMs. Describes how to systematically test for harmful outputs, prompt injection, and jailbreak vulnerabilities.
key_findings: Structured red teaming scales beyond ad-hoc testing
Multi-turn conversations reveal more vulnerabilities than single-turn
Red teaming should cover both direct and indirect attack surfaces

title: Scalable and Transferable Black-Box Jailbreaks for Language Models via Persona Modulation
summary: Introduces persona modulation as a social-engineering attack vector. Shows that assigning specific personas to LLMs dramatically increases jailbreak success rates.
key_findings: Persona-based jailbreaks are highly transferable across models
Authority figures and technical roles are most effective for jailbreaking
Automated persona search can discover new effective personas

title: Exploiting Programmatic Behavior of LLMs: Dual-Use Technologies for Security Attacks
summary: Shows how LLM tool-use and API integration patterns can be exploited. Demonstrates attacks where LLMs are tricked into executing malicious code or leaking data through their tool-calling capabilities.
key_findings: LLM tool-calling patterns introduce new attack surfaces
Agent frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT) amplify the impact of prompt injection
Defense requires input sanitization at every tool boundary
